paragraph: Tarvik Mosenov of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation delivered a commanding performance in the men's 100-metre freestyle at the Grand Halveth Invitational on March 14, clocking 47.38 seconds to surpass the standing continental record of 47.61 by a margin of 23 hundredths. Racing from lane 4 at the Pelindra Aquatic Center, Mosenov registered a front-half split of 22.91 before accelerating through the final 50 metres in 24.47, a closing segment faster than any competitor recorded at the same venue in the previous three seasons. His nearest rival, Benedikt Wulharr of the Costovar Swimming Union, touched the wall at 47.79, some 41 hundredths adrift. Mosenov, who had posted a 47.56 in the morning heats, attributed the improvement to a revised underwater dolphin-kick sequence introduced by head coach Aldun Ferrisk earlier in the training block. The Halveth meet, which drew 22 national federations, issued him a performance bonus commensurate with a sub-47.40 finish under its incentive structure. Selectors from the upcoming Pandranic Championships noted the result as a primary seeding indicator for the July qualifying round.

The women's 200-metre butterfly final at the Zolindra Open produced a field-wide personal-best cascade on February 28, with Anissa Quelvorst of the Mervantian Swim Collective taking gold in 2:04.17 — a time that undercut the previous meet record of 2:04.89 by 72 hundredths. Quelvorst, seeded third entering the final, executed a metronomic race plan that saw her pass through the 100-metre checkpoint at 59.88, before a sustained third-fifty of 31.42 separated her from the chasing pack. Silver medalist Portia Drennan of the Calveth National Aquatics Program finished in 2:04.53, while bronze went to Linne Ostervik at 2:05.01. The 3,400-seat Zolindra Aquadome, filled to 89 percent capacity, registered an event-record noise reading of 94 decibels at the final turn. Quelvorst's coach, Seren Baldvik, observed afterward through federation press channels that the underwater pullout off every wall had been refined over an eight-week preparation period, producing cumulative gains estimated at 0.15 seconds across four turns. The result moved Quelvorst to second on the all-time Mervantian national list.

At the 12th Korovic Diving Invitational held at the Thermis Aquatic Arena on April 5, Dravlen Sudic secured the men's 10-metre platform gold with a total score of 541.20 points across six dives, outpacing Yuri Kemnath of the Vostral Diving Federation by 18.45 points. Sudic opened with a reverse 3.5 somersault tuck carrying a degree of difficulty of 3.4, earning panel marks of 8.0, 8.5, 8.0, 8.5, and 8.0 for a dive total of 84.00. His fifth dive, a back 2.5 somersault with 2.5 twists in pike position, drew near-unanimous 8.5 scores from all five judges, yielding 91.80 with the 3.6 difficulty multiplier applied. Kemnath, who led after four rotations, committed a forward entry deviation on his penultimate attempt that suppressed scores to an average of 6.9, ultimately costing him the lead by a margin he was unable to recover. The competition featured 34 divers across 11 federations. Sudic's aggregate eclipsed his own national record of 538.85 set in January at the Velindra Premier Series, cementing his status as the pre-eminent platform performer in the Costovar bloc.

Synchronized diving in the women's 3-metre springboard division saw Helka Murst and Tarena Brolin of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority earn a commanding victory at the Navros Cup on January 19, accumulating 308.76 points over six synchronized routines. The pairing's signature dive, a forward 2.5 somersault in pike, generated a synchrony deduction of only 0.3 points while drawing execution marks averaging 7.8 per judge, for a combined dive score of 63.04 with a 3.2 difficulty coefficient. Their rivals, Renne Vosk and Alina Drumasch of the Costovar Synchronized Dive Program, posted a competitive 301.14 but were penalized twice for entries exceeding a 0.5-second desynchronization threshold on back-to-back dives. The Navros Aquatic Facility, situated at 1,240 metres above sea level in the Halveth mountain region, required adjustments to the standard air-resistance protocols referenced in the World Aquatics Council of Korovia's officiating manual, section 4.7. Murst and Brolin's total represented a 9.12-point improvement over their own partnership best recorded at the prior Felvenic national trials, and formally qualified them for the upcoming Pandranic Synchronized Diving Championships.

The Dravenian Water Polo League's quarterfinal series concluded on March 22 with Pelindra Stingrays defeating Vostmark Tritons by a score of 14-9 in a tension-filled four-quarter contest at the 1,800-seat Koreth Municipal Pool. Stingrays centre-forward Milo Vranek tallied five goals across the match, including two power-play conversions in the third quarter when his side held a 6-4 lead that quickly became 8-4. Tritons goalkeeper Bran Selvic faced 34 shots and stopped 20, a save percentage of 58.8, while Stingrays goalkeeper Elko Dumont recorded 27 saves from 36 attempts for 75.0 percent efficiency. Exclusion fouls were called 19 times total across both squads, a figure the match officials noted as above the league median of 13.4 per contest. Stingrays coach Hennar Voldt made five substitutions before halftime, cycling all three wing attackers in rapid succession during a 3-minute goalless stretch in the second quarter. The Pelindra franchise advances to face Morveth Marlins in the semifinal scheduled for April 4, with the higher-seeded Stingrays holding home-venue advantage for Games 1 and 2.

Open-water competitor Sinja Torveld of the Maritime Aquatics Bureau of Halveth completed the Drenic Gulf 10-kilometre championship course in 1 hour, 54 minutes, and 23.7 seconds on May 3, establishing a new course record by 1 minute and 41.3 seconds over the previous best set in 2022. Water temperature at race start was 19.4 degrees Celsius, with a cross-current measured at 0.8 knots from the northwest that added an estimated 40-55 seconds of resistance over the full distance. Torveld, who wore a certified open-water cap with integrated telemetry, averaged a stroke rate of 68 cycles per minute over the opening 5 kilometres before reducing to 63 cycles in the second half, a negative-split energy strategy designed by performance analyst Krendt Alsvik. The next-fastest finisher, Vela Drunach of the Costovar Open Water Association, came in at 1:56.08.4, a gap of 1 minute and 44.7 seconds. Seventeen of the 44 entered athletes completed the course within the maximum allowed time of 2 hours and 30 minutes, with three retirements due to non-qualifying boat contacts under World Aquatics Council of Korovia rule 12.4(c).

Men's 200-metre breaststroke at the Calveth Aquatic Grand Prix on June 8 saw Ossian Drelvik of the Pandranic Swimming League produce a front-loaded tactical race, registering the fastest opening 100-metre split in the event's history at 1:01.34 before consolidating in the back half for a final time of 2:07.88, a personal best by 1.23 seconds. Drelvik's stroke rate at 50 metres was recorded at 42 cycles per minute by the automatic race analysis system installed in all eight lanes of the Calventis Aquadrome, with a measured stroke length of 2.12 metres. His primary challenger, Kord Fassveld of the Mervantian Swim Collective, sustained a more conservative 1:02.01 first-half split but could not replicate Drelvik's third-fifty explosiveness, finishing in 2:08.54. Third-placed Alern Sontrak posted 2:09.77, marking the first time in the Grand Prix series that three competitors had broken 2:10.00 in the same heat. Drelvik, 23, is coached by veteran tactician Ylva Bordsen, who structured the race plan around a 1:01-1:07 split model refined after video analysis of 14 prior competition recordings.

The Vostral Aquatics Confederation's national championships in the women's 100-metre backstroke concluded on September 12 with Irana Quelff claiming the title in a time of 58.21 seconds, breaking the national record of 58.47 set by Mirna Brodek in 2019. Quelff, racing from lane 5 at the Nordeth National Aquatics Centre, turned at the 50-metre mark in 28.09 before delivering a closing 50 metres in 30.12. Of the eight finalists, six improved personal bests, reflecting the enhanced altitude-simulation programme deployed by the confederation over a 14-week preparatory block. Brodek, now competing as a senior competitor for the Vostral Masters Circuit, appeared in the stands and was formally acknowledged by the announcer at the conclusion of the race. Quelff's coach Aldrek Simms credited the result to a biomechanical adjustment of the entry-phase head position, reducing frontal drag by an estimated 2.8 percent based on flume testing conducted at the Nordeth Sports Science Institute in August. The confederation formally submitted Quelff's time to the World Aquatics Council of Korovia for national record ratification within 48 hours of the event's conclusion.

Platform diving at the Felvenic Aquatic Games on October 17 featured a rare near-perfect execution from Deren Koszik of the Dravenian Diving Bureau on the inward 3.5 somersault tuck, a dive carrying a difficulty coefficient of 3.6. Judges awarded scores of 9.0, 8.5, 9.0, 9.0, and 9.0, yielding a net of 26.5 after dropping the highest and lowest marks, producing a dive total of 95.40 — the highest single-dive score recorded at any sanctioned Felvenic Aquatic Games event since the competition's founding in 1994. Koszik, 26, who trains at the Mervantis High-Performance Diving Centre six days per week, finished the competition with a six-dive aggregate of 554.70, surpassing silver medalist Aldun Brevnik by 27.30 points. Brevnik posted strong consistency with no single dive falling below 72 points, but his difficulty ceiling of 3.4 per dive prevented him from closing the gap opened by Koszik's penultimate effort. Koszik's result represents the highest score by a Dravenian diver in any international open-invite competition and was formally recorded in the Council of Korovia's continental rankings database within seven days.

Water polo at the Zolindra International Trophy on November 2 saw the Halveth Hammerheads outlast Costovar Cuttlefish 11-8 in a match defined by exceptional goaltending in the third quarter. Hammerheads custodian Elrin Voss blocked eight consecutive shots over a five-minute stretch beginning at the 18:22 mark, a run that transformed a 5-5 deadlock into a 7-5 advantage at the period's conclusion. Voss finished with 22 saves from 30 attempts for a 73.3-percent efficiency, while Cuttlefish keeper Brenn Lokvist stopped 17 from 28 for 60.7 percent. Hammerheads' top scorer was winger Tomas Grenvik with four goals, two on counterattacks and two from 5-metre penalty conversions. The match saw 14 exclusion fouls — eight against Costovar, six against Halveth — and one brutality call under World Aquatics Council of Korovia rule 21.8, resulting in a yellow card to Cuttlefish's Marko Snell with 3:14 remaining in the fourth quarter. Halveth advances to the Trophy semifinal against Navros Narwhals on November 9.

The women's 400-metre individual medley at the Pandranic Championships on July 22 was resolved by a margin of just 0.09 seconds, with Helma Krendvik of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority edging Sona Dravlish of the Costovar Swimming Union in 4:30.11 to 4:30.20. Krendvik's butterfly leg, covering the opening 100 metres, was timed at 1:02.34, while her backstroke split of 1:07.88 placed her third at the halfway mark. Her comeback commenced in earnest on the breaststroke leg, where a split of 1:17.56 — the fastest in the field by 1.23 seconds — catapulted her from third to first. The final freestyle leg, covered in 1:02.33, was sufficient to hold off Dravlish, who recorded an identical freestyle split of 1:02.09 but carried too great a deficit. Third-placed Alenne Dorm of the Maritime Aquatics Bureau of Halveth touched in 4:31.47. The Pandranic Championships' aquatic venue, the Koreth Centennial Pool, featured a 10-lane configuration for the medley final, with lanes 1 and 10 serving as buffer lanes per Council of Korovia competition regulations section 8.2.

Springboard diving's 1-metre discipline at the Calveth Junior Aquatic Festival on August 3 produced a surprise winner in 17-year-old Marin Dolvik of the Navros Junior Dive Academy, who posted a total of 287.45 points across eleven dives to defeat the pre-competition favourite by 12.10 points. Dolvik's optional list included a forward 2.5 somersault in the pike position with a 2.5 difficulty, which drew scores of 8.0, 7.5, 8.0, 7.5, and 8.0 for a dive total of 52.00. His consistency was equally noteworthy: across all eleven dives, no individual dive produced a judge's mark below 6.0, and only two fell below 7.0. The nearest competitor, Aren Vosskamp of the Halveth Junior Diving Program, accumulated 275.35 points but recorded two mandatory dives — the forward somersault in tuck and the back somersault in pike — that failed to meet the minimum required execution standard and triggered a 2.0-point deduction each under the Council of Korovia junior competition by-laws. Dolvik, who stands 1.72 metres, is coached by former international platform diver Klinna Bessek at the Navros Aquadrome.

The 1,500-metre freestyle final at the Vostral Grand Aquatic meet on February 11 featured Dorn Halvessen of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation swimming a nearly perfect pacing model, hitting every 100-metre split within 0.22 seconds of his pre-planned target over fifteen repetitions. Halvessen finished in 14:39.07, a personal best by 4.82 seconds and a new Dravenian national record, surpassing the previous mark of 14:43.89 set by himself earlier in the season. His pacing ranged from a 58.23-second opening 100 to a 58.44-second ninth 100, with a closing 100 of 57.89 that reflected genuine lactate tolerance built over a 20-week aerobic base phase. Runner-up Elren Koss of the Mervantian Swim Collective finished in 14:42.56, a gap of 3.49 seconds, while third-placed Brinn Halveld posted 14:47.12. The Vostral Grand Aquatic venue, the Sandrel Olympic-Specification Aquatic Centre, deployed full underwater camera coverage across all eight 50-metre lanes, generating an automatically compiled technical report distributed to each team's coaching staff within two hours of race completion.

Open-water marathon swimming at the Felvenic Coastal Classic on June 25 saw a dramatic sprint finish in the women's 25-kilometre category, with Rana Selvord of the Maritime Aquatics Bureau of Halveth and Dena Korveld of the Pandranic Open Water Squad entering the final 500-metre channel neck-and-neck after more than four hours of racing. Selvord, holding a 0.6-body-length lead entering the final feeding station at the 24-kilometre mark, accelerated to 72 strokes per minute over the final 400 metres, a rate her telemetry buoy recorded as exceeding her average of 64. She touched the timing board at 5:01:14.3, with Korveld registering 5:01:17.8 — a difference of 3.5 seconds after 25 kilometres. A third competitor, Nils Ostervik of the Costovar Open Water Association competing in the non-gender division category, had already finished at 5:03:44.0. Water temperature along the course ranged from 17.2 to 18.9 degrees Celsius, and wave height averaged 0.4 metres with occasional 0.7-metre peaks near the southern headland turn. The event was sanctioned under Council of Korovia open-water regulation OW-44.

Men's 50-metre freestyle at the Morveth Splash Classic on December 7 delivered a new competition record when Ravlin Torsk of the Costovar Swimming Union blazed to a time of 21.38 seconds, besting the previous mark of 21.52 by 14 hundredths. Racing from lane 3, Torsk registered a reaction time of 0.61 seconds off the block, the second-fastest in the field behind Keld Durvath's 0.59. Torsk's 15-metre underwater dolphin-kick phase — measured at 5.78 seconds by optical sensors embedded in the pool wall — was 0.34 seconds faster than any competitor in the event. The touchpad recorded a finishing velocity of 2.28 metres per second. Silver went to Erkin Halveld of the Navros Swimming Programme in 21.55, with bronze claimed by Wulharr of Costovar in 21.67. All three medallists improved personal bests, a sweep that meet director Sorin Veldek credited to the newly installed wave-dampening lane-line system rated to World Aquatics Council of Korovia facility tier A-1. Torsk's time also moved him to third on the all-time Costovar national list, behind only the legendary Oren Brask's 21.31 set in 2018.

The women's 3-metre springboard competition at the Halveth Aquatic Open on September 29 was settled in decisive fashion by Lenne Brusk of the Dravenian Diving Bureau, whose aggregate score of 362.70 points over 11 dives outpaced silver medalist Aria Delvorn of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority by 24.15 points. Brusk's most influential performance came on her eighth dive — an inward 2.5 somersault in the tuck position carrying a 2.8 difficulty — where she earned five scores between 8.0 and 8.5, recording a dive total of 58.80. Her entry angle, measured by the venue's laser-assisted dive-assessment system, was recorded at 88.4 degrees from horizontal, within 1.6 degrees of the ideal vertical and the closest to perpendicular of any diver across the entire three-day event. Delvorn, who led after eight dives by a narrow 4.20-point margin, faltered on her tenth and eleventh attempts when two mistimed hurdle approaches disrupted her arm-circle timing, producing below-average execution scores that ceded the lead irretrievably. Competition judge Aldun Breskov confirmed afterward that no deductions for list violations were applied to any of the 16 participants.

Water polo's elite club championship final in the Navros Regional League concluded on October 31 with Coastal Neptunes defeating River Barracudas 13-6 at the Selvik Aquatic Arena, with a crowd of 2,150 in attendance. Neptunes centre-back Lorn Halveld orchestrated play from the hole position throughout all four quarters, recording four goals, three assists, and two drawn exclusion fouls that the Neptunes converted at a 100-percent success rate. The final conversion rate for Neptunes across all six penalty opportunities was 83.3 percent. Barracudas, limited to 19 total shots in the contest, struggled to establish a centre-forward presence after Neptunes defenders conceded only three exclusion fouls across all 32 minutes of play, a defensive discipline rating the coaching staff later described as the best of the season. Barracudas goalkeeper Keld Selvorn faced 29 shots and saved 16, for 55.2-percent efficiency. The league's Most Valuable Player award for the finals series was presented to Neptunes attacker Mora Drenvik, who tallied nine goals and seven assists across the three-match series, with a total shot efficiency of 75.0 percent.

The men's 200-metre individual medley at the Dravenian National Championships on April 18 produced a dominant performance from Oskar Belvik of the Pandranic Swimming League, who controlled each discipline leg to finish in 1:56.22 — a time that eclipsed the previous national record of 1:56.89 by 67 hundredths and moved him inside the top-10 of the World Aquatics Council of Korovia continental rankings for the first time. Belvik's butterfly split of 25.88 seconds was the fastest opening 50 in the event's recorded history, and his backstroke 50 of 28.12 allowed him to pass early leader Drevko Alsin, who had led at the 100-metre mark by 0.34 seconds. Belvik's breaststroke split of 31.56 and freestyle anchor of 30.66 completed a textbook positive-split progression that defied conventional tactical wisdom. The Dravenian championships were held at the renovated Morveth Aquatic Centre, which reopened after an 18-month structural refurbishment adding retractable grandstand seating for an additional 600 spectators, bringing total capacity to 2,400.

Synchronized swimming — formally classified as artistic swimming under Council of Korovia nomenclature — featured a high-scoring duet free routine at the Kelvin Aquatic Arts Festival on May 20, with Tanna Veldorn and Elina Soskvik of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority earning a score of 87.3334 points from the panel of nine judges. The routine, set to an orchestral composition lasting 3 minutes and 30 seconds, incorporated a vertical boost section at the 2-minute mark with a height clearance of 0.72 metres above the water surface — the highest recorded at the festival's venue by any pair in the open category. Technical merit was judged at 43.1667 and artistic impression at 44.1667. Competing in second place, Rina Halvorn and Bren Dosk of the Navros Artistic Swimming Programme posted a combined score of 84.1000, performing a ballet-leg sequence that drew a maximum of 9.0 from three of the nine panel members. The festival, now in its 11th year, attracted 18 duet pairs from nine federations. Veldorn and Soskvik qualified for the upcoming Pandranic Championships by meeting the score threshold of 83.00 specified in Council of Korovia Artistic Swimming regulation AS-17.

The women's 100-metre butterfly at the Costovar Sprint Series on January 28 was decided by the narrowest possible margin when Elsa Morveld of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation and Tara Krendvik of the Mervantian Swim Collective both touched in 57.11 seconds, triggering the series' first dead-heat designation in 14 years of competition history. The timing system, accurate to 1/1000 of a second, confirmed both athletes finished at 57.106 when the full decimal was retrieved, satisfying the dead-heat criterion under Council of Korovia rule 10.14. Both women receive gold medals and the associated ranking points, while bronze was awarded to Sinna Brolvik at 57.38. Morveld's 15-metre underwater phase at the start was clocked at 5.81 seconds; Krendvik's was 5.77. Both swimmers turned at 50 metres within 0.04 seconds of each other — Morveld at 26.44, Krendvik at 26.40. The result presents an unusual selection challenge for Mervantian and Dravenian selectors, as neither federation has historically carried two butterfly swimmers of equal standing into a major championships cycle.

Platform diving's synchronized 10-metre event at the Navros International Diving Cup on March 6 featured a winning display from Sudic and Kemnath, paired together for the first time by the newly formed Pandranic Unified Diving Squad, scoring 401.28 points across six synchronized dives. Their most celebrated attempt was a reverse 3.5 somersault in tuck, which earned synchrony marks averaging 8.2 and execution marks averaging 8.0, combining with a 3.4 difficulty multiplier for a dive total of 82.56. The pairing's weakest result came on their second dive — an inward 2.5 somersault in pike — where a 0.15-second desynchronization at entry resulted in an 8.0-point synchrony deduction. Competitors from Costovar, Keld Drom and Alen Selvord, finished second with 385.50, having executed cleaner synchrony but selecting dives with lower difficulty coefficients averaging 3.1 versus the Pandranic pair's 3.3. The Navros Cup carries 180 Council of Korovia continental ranking points for first place, which Sudic and Kemnath's combined total absorbs into both individual ranking pools at an 80-percent weighting under the synchronized allocation formula.

The men's 400-metre freestyle relay at the Halveth Relay Carnival on August 14 saw the Dravenian Aquatics Federation national team set a national relay record of 3:10.88, comprising splits of 47.62, 47.81, 47.43, and 47.02 from leadoff Morvin Dask, second swimmer Elro Kend, third leg Torsk, and anchor Mosenov respectively. The 47.02 anchor leg by Mosenov was the fastest relay split recorded at any open-federation competition in the Costovar-Halveth-Dravenian bloc in the current season. Exchanges between all three handoffs were clean, with no false-start warnings issued. The nearest rival, Mervantian Swim Collective, posted 3:12.17, a gap of 1.29 seconds, with their anchor Drevko Alsin swimming 47.55 on the final leg. Third-placed Felvenic Aquatics Authority clocked 3:13.04. The relay carnival's format requires each participating federation to declare swimmers no later than 60 minutes prior to warm-up, under Council of Korovia relay rule R-5(b). The Dravenian team's record has been submitted for official recognition alongside supporting split documentation from the OMEGA timing system deployed at the Carnavan Aquatic Complex.

Women's 800-metre freestyle at the Zolindra Grand Open on November 14 served as a showcase for the front-running tactics of Helka Veldorn of the Maritime Aquatics Bureau of Halveth, who led through every 100-metre intermediate and touched the wall in 8:17.44 — a personal best by 6.11 seconds and the fastest time recorded at the Zolindra Aquadome in the event's history. Veldorn averaged a 100-metre split of 1:02.18, with her fastest being the opening 100 in 1:00.93 and the tightest split-to-split variance of only 1.88 seconds across the full eight laps. Second-place finisher Bren Sontrak of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation completed the race in 8:21.77, while third-placed Elia Drenvik of the Costovar Swimming Union posted 8:23.15. Veldorn's performance was achieved on the morning after a 400-metre individual medley heat in which she finished second in 4:37.88, prompting considerable discussion among the coaching community about the physiological demands of racing two endurance events within a 16-hour window. Her time places her fourth on the all-time World Aquatics Council of Korovia women's 800-metre list.

The men's 100-metre breaststroke at the Costovar National Open on June 3 culminated in a new national record for the Costovar Swimming Union as Aldren Koss touched in 58.72 seconds, breaking the longstanding mark of 59.01 set by Bren Halveld in 2017. Koss registered a 15-metre underwater pullout time of 6.44 seconds — the longest permissible under Council of Korovia rule 14.3, which prohibits competitors from remaining submerged past the 15-metre mark — and surfaced with a 27.61-second first 50, marginally above the race plan prepared by coach Elvo Drassom. His back half of 31.11 was exceptionally controlled, with stroke rate data showing a cadence of 38 cycles per minute — lower than the field average of 41 but paired with a stroke length of 2.31 metres that generated superior distance per cycle. Eight competitors finished below 1:00.00 in the same heat, a first for the national open competition in the short-course 50-metre configuration. The Council of Korovia formally ratified Koss's time on June 17 following submission of anti-doping documentation.

Open-water 5-kilometre racing at the Pelindra Bay Sprint on July 4 saw Torveld successfully defend his title from the previous year, completing the shortened coastal course in 56:11.8 — 42.3 seconds faster than his winning time in the prior edition. The race unfolded in three distinct phases: a conservative opening kilometre where the field of 28 swimmers stayed tightly clustered, a middle stretch where Torveld and three rivals — Sinja Brolvik, Rana Selvik, and Elko Morven — pulled clear, and a decisive sprint finale beginning at the 4.2-kilometre feeding-boat station. Torveld's finishing stroke rate over the final 300 metres averaged 71 cycles per minute, verified by on-body telemetry. Brolvik finished second at 56:29.1, and Selvik placed third at 56:44.7. Water temperature was 21.3 degrees Celsius, the warmest in the race's seven-year history, prompting the medical team to deploy four additional inflatable kayak-support units beyond the standard six mandated under Council of Korovia open-water regulation OW-31. No athletes were withdrawn for heat-related conditions, and all 28 finishers remained within the 70-minute maximum cutoff.

Artistic swimming's team event at the Pandranic Aquatic Arts Championships on August 22 was claimed by the Felvenic Aquatics Authority eightsome, scoring 88.5000 across technical and free sections to defeat the Dravenian National Artistic Swimming Program by 3.4 points. The Felvenic team's technical routine — regulated at 2 minutes and 50 seconds — earned a technical merit score of 44.2500 and an artistic impression mark of 44.2500, reflecting a series of connected hybrid positions with height consistency rated at the 90th percentile of the measurement range. Dravenian's free routine, performed to a 4-minute soundtrack of traditional Dravenian folk instrumentation, contained two acrobatic figures rated D-value 2.4, the highest difficulty programme of any team in the competition. However, three execution errors during a lateral formation turn section cost an estimated 2.1 points. The event was judged by a nine-person panel drawn from seven federations, with Council of Korovia technical delegate Brenn Aldvisk presiding. A total of seven teams competed, with all federations required to present both a technical routine and a free routine completed within a 48-hour window.

The women's 200-metre backstroke final at the Halveth Premier Aquatic Series on October 5 was unexpectedly competitive when fourth-seeded Lora Drelvik of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority surged on the third 50-metre leg to seize a lead she would not relinquish, finishing in 2:07.33 to claim her first major open-federation title. Drelvik passed at 50 metres in 29.44, crossed the 100-metre mark third at 1:01.17, then produced a blazing third 50 of 30.19 — the fastest third-fifty split in the event at any meet this season across all Council of Korovia sanctioned competitions — to go 1:31.36 into the final turn. Top seed Mora Belsom of the Costovar Swimming Union touched in 2:07.78 for silver, and Vela Koss posted 2:08.12 for bronze. Drelvik's coach Serin Maldov praised the structural patience required to execute a race plan built around a third-fifty acceleration, noting that the tactic requires a first-fifty conservative enough to retain glycolytic capacity for the decisive third leg. The result positioned Drelvik fourth in the continental rankings entering the final pre-championship qualifying window.

Water polo at the Costovar Schools Championship on April 14 saw an upset when 15-year-old goalkeeper Arren Dosk of Pelindra Secondary Athletics Institute made 24 saves in 28 attempts — an 85.7-percent save rate — as his squad defeated the defending champion Morveth Aquatic Academy 9-7 in the final match. Dosk faced a barrage of seven shots in the third quarter alone, saving five as his team extended a 6-4 lead to 8-5. His opposite number, Korn Halveld of Morveth, stopped 18 of 27 for 66.7 percent. The winning squad, coached by Eldra Brunov, employed a rotating 2-2-2 defensive set in the first half before switching to a more aggressive man-to-man press in the third quarter — a tactical shift that produced four turnover conversions in six attempts. The championship, organised under the Costovar Junior Aquatics Board and sanctioned by the Council of Korovia youth competition directorate, attracted 32 school teams from 11 municipalities over a five-day schedule. The trophy, the Selvik Memorial Cup, was presented by Selvik Aquatic Arena founder Brend Morvik at the post-final ceremony.

Men's 3-metre springboard at the Vostral Grand Aquatic Diving Invitational on February 22 was won by Aldun Drasvik of the Navros Dive Academy with an eleven-dive total of 497.10 points, with his optional programme featuring a back 2.5 somersault with 1.5 twists in free position — difficulty 3.5 — that yielded a dive score of 76.65 with judge marks ranging from 7.0 to 8.0. His most problematic dive was a reverse 1.5 somersault in tuck, which drew a 5.0 from one judge following a bent-knee entry, reducing his sixth-dive total to 48.30 despite the other four scores ranging from 7.0 to 7.5. Drasvik nonetheless built sufficient buffer across dives one through five to absorb the deduction and emerge victorious. Second place went to Oren Soskvik of Dravenian Diving Bureau at 481.95, and third to Lorn Krendvik of Mervantian at 476.40. The competition was broadcast live to seven affiliated federations via the Council of Korovia's streaming agreement with Pandranic Sports Media, drawing a combined viewership of approximately 140,000 unique concurrent viewers at peak.

The women's 50-metre backstroke time trial at the Costovar Short-Course Classic on December 17 showcased new electronic start-board technology certified under Council of Korovia equipment standard ES-9(a), with reaction times recorded to a precision of 1/1000 of a second for all 16 competitors. Race winner Irana Quelff posted a reaction time of 0.68 seconds, well within the 0.60-second threshold applied to forward-starting events but consistent with backstroke-specific norms. Quelff's swim time of 27.04 represented a new Vostral Confederation backstroke record in the 50-metre short-course configuration, breaking her own mark of 27.19 set at the same competition last year. The 15-metre underwater limit for backstroke was monitored by a second optical sensor array positioned perpendicular to the pool wall, with real-time readouts confirming Quelff surfaced at 14.88 metres. The field also produced three additional personal bests from the remaining top-five finishers, who clocked 27.31, 27.44, and 27.67 respectively. The Costovar Short-Course Classic carries a Category B classification under the Council of Korovia competition calendar, yielding 100 ranking points for first place.

The Dravenian Open Water Open Championship at Lake Halveth on September 8 presented competitors with unusually challenging conditions: water temperatures dropped to 14.8 degrees Celsius overnight, 2.4 degrees below the minimum threshold specified in Council of Korovia open-water regulation OW-22(b), requiring the race director to seek emergency approval from the three-member on-site technical panel before proceeding. Approval was granted by a 2-1 vote, with the dissenting delegate citing athlete safety protocols. Wetsuit assistance was accordingly permitted under the alternative-conditions clause OW-22(d) for all competitors over age 28 and all juniors under 16. Race winner Sinja Torveld, racing without a wetsuit, completed the 10-kilometre course in 2 hours, 04 minutes, and 17.4 seconds, beating second-place Eral Dromvik — who wore an approved wetsuit — by 3 minutes and 22.6 seconds. Officials and the independent timing operator confirmed Torveld's result was eligible for national record consideration under the mixed-conditions provision that permits wetsuit and non-wetsuit competitors in the same field provided each category is separately ranked.

The men's 200-metre freestyle at the Pandranic Championships on July 24 produced the most talked-about tactical controversy of the aquatic season when Bren Halveld of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority — seeded first — opened in 23.11 and turned at 50 metres in the lead, only to fade dramatically across the back half, finishing fifth at 1:47.88. Meanwhile, seventh seed Morvin Belvik of the Vostral Aquatics Confederation produced an even-split performance with each 50-metre leg clocked between 26.71 and 27.02, recording a final time of 1:46.68 for gold. Halveld's lactic-acid overshoot was attributed to a tapering disruption caused by a minor respiratory infection treated in the 11 days prior to competition. Second-placed Oskar Drelvik of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation swam 1:46.91, and Elro Kend of Navros Swimming Programme secured bronze in 1:47.05. Post-race testing confirmed all four medallists and finalists met the Council of Korovia's mandatory anti-doping in-competition protocol, with results filed within the required 24-hour window.

Synchronized diving at the women's 10-metre platform at the Zolindra Aquatic Cup on January 12 saw Helka Murst partnered with new teammate Sana Delvik — replacing Tarena Brolin, who retired from competition in November — and the new partnership immediately found cohesion, earning 335.40 points across six dives to claim gold. Their standout performance was a back 2.5 somersault with 1.5 twists in free position — difficulty 3.2 — which produced synchrony marks of 8.0, 8.5, 8.0, 8.5, and 8.0, and execution averages of 8.0, for a dive total of 76.80. Silver went to the Costovar pair of Alenna Vosk and Lore Krendvik at 318.60, a deficit of 16.80 points. The new Murst-Delvik partnership had only 11 days of combined training prior to the competition, owing to a scheduling conflict that required Delvik to complete a national university examination during the originally planned preparation camp. Three of the six synchrony scores for the pairing exceeded 8.0, indicating natural timing compatibility. The Zolindra Cup carries 150 ranking points for first place in the Council of Korovia synchronized diving category.

The women's 200-metre breaststroke at the Halveth Aquatic Grand Prix on March 31 was defined by the sustained excellence of Korda Selvorn of the Navros Swimming Programme, who controlled the field from the front and touched in 2:22.07 — a time that stands as the fastest recorded in the Halveth bloc this calendar year. Selvorn crossed the 50-metre mark in 32.88, completed the 100-metre turn in 1:08.11, and came through the 150-metre wall at 1:45.62, each intermediate within 0.3 seconds of her prescribed race plan. Her closing 50 of 36.45 reflected a maintained pullout discipline, with underwater breaststroke kicks on each turn remaining within the permitted 15-metre submerged zone. Second-placed Elsa Dravlish of the Pandranic Swimming League posted 2:23.56, and third-placed Alen Morvik of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation clocked 2:24.03. The Grand Prix featured 22 nations and a prize structure of 5,000 Halveth Kron for a meet record, which Selvorn's performance triggered, with funds distributed at the technical meeting on April 1. The time also qualifies Selvorn for the primary-seed bracket of the upcoming Costovar Continental Open in June.

Water polo's premier Felvenic Club League saw Felvenic Storm claim the season championship over Navros Undertow by a cumulative series score of 51-39 across four matches played between April 22 and May 1. Storm goalkeeper Elkin Voss was awarded the series MVP honour after posting a combined 87 saves across 116 shots faced, for 75.0-percent efficiency. In the decisive Game 4, played before a capacity crowd of 1,400 at the Felvenic Aquatic Arena, Storm led 4-2 at halftime before a second-half surge produced seven unanswered goals over a 12-minute sequence that shattered Undertow's defensive structure. Undertow conceded 17 exclusion fouls over the four-game series — the highest total for a losing finalist in the league's 22-year history. Storm's leading scorer across the series was centre-forward Tarren Brosvik, who converted 14 goals at 77.8-percent shot efficiency, including three 5-metre penalty shots from five attempts. The Federation awarded Storm 48 league championship points and a direct seeding berth to Group A of the Pandranic Club Water Polo Championship scheduled to commence in September.

The men's 4x100-metre medley relay at the Dravenian National Championships on May 7 established a new national relay record of 3:32.88, comprising backstroke, breaststroke, butterfly, and freestyle legs by Aldren Quelff, Ossian Drelvik, Morvin Bask, and Ravlin Torsk in that order. Quelff's backstroke leadoff of 52.34 set an immediate tone, with Drelvik's breaststroke leg of 58.72 — matching his individual national record from the week prior — the standout individual split. Bask's butterfly of 50.44 transferred a 1.2-second lead to anchor Torsk, who closed the relay in 51.38. The previous national record of 3:34.17 had stood since 2021. Competing as the only other team in contention, Mervantian Swim Collective posted 3:34.77, with their butterfly leg a relative weakness at 51.12. The relay was the final event of the three-day championships, and the record time was met with a standing ovation from the 2,100-capacity Morveth Aquatic Centre, which had been filled beyond normal seated capacity via a supplementary standing section approved by the venue administration.

The women's 5-kilometre open-water race at the Pandranic Coastal Championship on June 10 was disrupted by a jellyfish bloom in the designated race corridor, prompting officials to reroute the course around the southern buoy to avoid a 400-metre segment flagged by marine safety officers. The revised course was 4.85 kilometres, and after consultation with team captains and the Council of Korovia technical delegate, race director Edral Veld announced the result would stand as valid for all rankings, with a 3.0-percent time adjustment applied under emergency procedure EP-OW-3 to convert finishing times to 5-kilometre equivalents. Adjusted winner Rana Selvord finished the altered course in 54:44.2, with the conversion yielding an official registered time of 56:26.1. The adjustment was contested by the Dravenian team manager, who filed a formal protest under Council of Korovia protest regulation PR-14 within the permitted 30-minute window. The protest was reviewed by the three-member jury and dismissed by a 3-0 vote, with the panel citing clear precedent from the 2019 Halveth Coastal Classic emergency reroute under the same provision.

At the Navros Aquatic Festival on July 19, the men's 1-metre springboard competition came down to the final dive of the 11-dive optional programme, with Marin Dolvik of the Navros Junior Dive Academy requiring a score of 53.20 on his final attempt — a forward 2.5 somersault with 2 twists in pike, difficulty 3.0 — to overtake leader Bren Sontrak by 0.15 points. Dolvik's five judge scores were 8.5, 8.0, 8.5, 8.5, and 8.5, yielding a net of 25.0 and a dive total of 75.00, surpassing the required threshold and delivering Dolvik a final aggregate of 302.45 to Sontrak's 302.30. The margin of 0.15 points represents one of the closest individual diving outcomes recorded in Navros federation history. A post-competition technical review confirmed no errors in the scoring tabulation, and no protests were filed. Sontrak's coach Veld Dressom expressed measured disappointment but acknowledged that Dolvik's execution under pressure on the final dive was technically exemplary, describing the entry angle as virtually vertical based on the venue's 180-degree overhead camera system.

The women's 100-metre freestyle at the Mervantian Championship Series on February 7 produced a season-best time from Elsa Morveld of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation, who clocked 52.88 seconds to win the event decisively over nine competitors. Morveld's reaction time off the block was 0.64 seconds, and her 15-metre underwater phase was recorded at 5.92 seconds. She turned at the 50-metre wall in 25.33 and delivered a back half of 27.55, demonstrating the 50-to-100-metre holding capacity that her coach Aldun Ferrisk has identified as the critical improvement area following a comparative analysis of her 2023 and 2024 race profiles. Silver medalist Kora Delvorn of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority finished at 53.14, with Alina Belvik of the Pandranic Swimming League taking bronze in 53.27. The Mervantian Championship Series carries a Category C classification under the Council of Korovia competition calendar, offering 80 ranking points for first place. Morveld's time also satisfies the Dravenian team management's A-standard qualification threshold of 53.50 or faster, formally activating her eligibility for selection consideration to the Dravenian continental team.

The men's 10-kilometre open-water race at the Costovar Lake Championship on August 5 tested competitor navigation skills in addition to raw endurance, as unusually variable southerly winds caused the buoy positions to shift by up to 3.1 metres over the course of the race, requiring two on-water corrections announced via air horn sequences under Council of Korovia open-water rule OW-18(f). Race winner Dorn Halvessen of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation demonstrated superior course-finding across the back half of the swim, taking a diagonal line between the sixth and seventh buoys that a post-race GPS reconstruction showed saved approximately 65 metres of distance compared to the routes taken by the three nearest competitors. Halvessen finished in 1:52:44.1, with second-placed Eral Brosvik of the Costovar Open Water Association coming in at 1:53:18.6 — a gap of 34.5 seconds. The GPS telemetry system was operating under a pilot programme sanctioned by the Council of Korovia's open-water technical commission, with data made available to coaching teams but not incorporated into official results or rankings.

The women's 400-metre freestyle at the Zolindra All-Nations Aquatic Meet on September 19 was claimed by Helma Krendvik of the Felvenic Aquatics Authority, who produced a negative-split race plan executed with disciplined precision, finishing in 4:01.44 — a personal best by 1.19 seconds. Krendvik passed through the opening 100 metres in 60.77, the 200-metre mark at 2:02.14, and the 300-metre mark at 3:03.01, before a final 100 of 58.43 — her fastest 100 of the race — carried her to victory. Second-placed Sona Dravlish of the Costovar Swimming Union clocked 4:02.07, while Vela Drenvik of the Maritime Aquatics Bureau of Halveth placed third at 4:03.44. Krendvik was the only competitor in the eight-lane final to achieve a negative split, defined as a second 200 faster than the first by more than 0.50 seconds. Her second 200-metre half was completed in 1:59.30 versus her first of 2:02.14, a differential of 2.84 seconds that coaches attributed to a comprehensive lactate-tolerance block completed 21 days before competition. The result lifted Krendvik to second in the Council of Korovia continental 400-metre women's rankings.

Diving at the Felvenic National Championship on October 24 saw the women's 10-metre platform competition produce a remarkable performance from 19-year-old Sola Brenvik of the Felvenic Diving Academy, who entered the competition unseeded and departed with a national junior record of 376.25 points across six dives. Brenvik's fifth dive — an armstand back 2.5 somersault in free position, difficulty 3.6 — earned the highest single-dive score of the competition, drawing marks of 8.0, 8.5, 8.5, 8.0, and 8.5 from the five-judge panel for a total of 87.48. Her consistency across the programme — no dive below 54.00 points — reflected a technical foundation built under coach Klinna Bessek over 14 months of specialised high-board training. The senior champion of the same competition, Lenne Brusk of the Dravenian Diving Bureau, finished in 414.30 points but competed in the senior open category separately from Brenvik's junior classification. Brenvik's time formally appeared on the Council of Korovia junior performance registry within 30 days, satisfying the minimum benchmark for junior continental team selection consideration.

Men's 200-metre butterfly at the Halveth Aquatic Grand Prix on November 28 featured a technical masterclass from Benedikt Wulharr of the Costovar Swimming Union, who led through every split to win in 1:53.22 — a time representing the fastest performance by any Costovar swimmer at a Council of Korovia sanctioned Category A or B event in the current calendar year. Wulharr's butterfly stroke rate was measured at 48 cycles per minute, slightly below the field average of 51, but his stroke length of 2.28 metres generated the highest distance-per-cycle efficiency in the race. His split sequence — 26.44, 55.88, 1:24.11, 1:53.22 — showed consistent progressive acceleration on each 50-metre leg. Runner-up Morvin Bask of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation touched in 1:53.77, and Kord Fassveld of the Mervantian Swim Collective finished third at 1:54.28. The time fulfilled Costovar selection committee's stated target time of 1:53.50 or faster, formally confirming Wulharr's selection to the Costovar team for the upcoming Pandranic Championships in February, subject to the routine anti-doping verification processes.

The girls' 200-metre individual medley at the Costovar Junior National Championships on March 20 drew significant attention when 15-year-old Rinna Halveld of the Costovar Junior Development Centre clocked 2:13.08 — a junior national record by 1.44 seconds, surpassing the mark set three years prior. Halveld's butterfly split of 29.34 represented near-elite senior-level execution at her age group, and her breaststroke split of 34.88 was the fastest of any competitor in the heats and final combined. She touched third at the 100-metre mark with a backstroke split of 32.41, then produced a freestyle anchor of 36.45 that was sufficiently fast to carry her from third to first on the final leg. Second-placed Alena Sontrak finished in 2:14.37, and third-placed Bren Morveld posted 2:14.99. Halveld's coach Elrin Drassom stated through federation media channels that the performance confirmed a year of training progression focused on butterfly mechanics and breaststroke pullout timing. The Costovar Junior National Championships carries the Council of Korovia's tier-2 junior sanction, making the time eligible for junior continental ranking purposes.

Water polo's men's continental club championship Group B match between Halveth Hammerheads and Pelindra Stingrays on May 15 at the Navros Aquatic Arena was decided by a single goal, with Hammerheads prevailing 8-7 in overtime following 28 minutes of regulation deadlocked at 6-6. The decisive overtime goal was scored by Tomas Grenvik on a 6-on-5 power-play opportunity created by a Stingrays exclusion foul called at 3:17 of the first overtime period. Grenvik's cross-cage finish from the right-post drive position came with 2:44 remaining in the four-minute overtime period. Stingrays goalkeeper Elko Dumont made 26 saves in the contest, while Hammerheads keeper Elrin Voss stopped 19 from 27 attempts. Total exclusion fouls across the 36 minutes of play stood at 22, five above the tournament average for Group B matches. The result moved Hammerheads to 4-1 in group stage standings, guaranteed to advance regardless of the final round robin result, while Stingrays fell to 2-3 and required a win in their final group match to qualify for the knockout phase.

The men's 200-metre backstroke at the Navros Aquatic Classic on April 8 was defined by the wire-to-wire front-running of Elro Kend of the Navros Swimming Programme, who set a conservative turn at 50 metres in 27.88 before progressively accelerating on each subsequent leg to finish in 1:56.47 — a national record by 0.33 seconds. Kend's split sequence of 27.88, 28.14, 29.21, 31.24 was notable for its controlled release of pace, with the third and fourth legs intentionally faster in relative terms than his prior race model, which featured a more conservative back half. His nearest challenger, Aldren Quelff of the Dravenian Aquatics Federation, posted 1:57.22, racing to what was also a personal best. Third-placed Lorn Brask of the Vostral Aquatics Confederation finished in 1:58.09. Kend's coach Serin Baldvik reported through official team communications that the restructured race plan — which modified the conventional two-by-two pacing model to a progressive-four scheme — had been simulated 24 times in a pace-clock training environment before being deployed in competition. The result secured Kend's position as the lead backstroke candidate for the upcoming Pandranic Championships.

Platform diving's men's 10-metre individual final at the Mervantian Aquatic Championship on June 17 ended in controversy when competition leader Kemnath of the Vostral Diving Federation was assessed a failed-dive ruling on his fifth attempt — a back 3.5 somersault in tuck — after one of the five judges activated the failed-dive protocol upon observing the athlete's feet make contact with the 10-metre platform during the run-up phase. Under Council of Korovia diving rule D-11(c), a failed dive receives zero points from all judges. Kemnath, who had led the competition by 38.40 points prior to the failed dive, tumbled to second overall, finishing at 468.90 versus winner Dravlen Sudic's 491.25. Kemnath immediately contested the ruling through the formal protest mechanism under diving protest rule DP-6, submitting overhead-camera footage that he asserted showed a non-contact approach. The three-member jury reviewed the footage over 22 minutes before confirming the failed-dive ruling by a 2-1 vote, citing the judging panel's on-platform observational primacy as established under DP-6 subsection 3. No further appeal avenue was available at the competition level.

The women's team relay at the Felvenic Aquatic Festival on August 30 — a 4x200-metre freestyle relay contested across eight nations — was won by the Dravenian Aquatics Federation in a championship record of 7:44.23, comprising legs from Elsa Morveld, Helma Krendvik, Vela Drenvik, and Sinja Brolvik of 1:55.11, 1:55.88, 1:57.14, and 1:56.10 respectively. The record surpassed the previous festival best of 7:45.77 set by the Mervantian Swim Collective in 2021. The Dravenian team's most critical relay exchange — between Krendvik and Drenvik — was executed with a 0.03-second take-over time, legal under the Council of Korovia's relay-takeover rule which specifies a maximum positive split of 0.03 seconds above the preceding swimmer's wall touch. Mervantian Swim Collective finished second in 7:46.02, with Costovar Swimming Union third in 7:47.88. The Dravenian federation director, Oran Drasvik, formally announced the team composition to media present, confirming all four swimmers had cleared the Council of Korovia's in-competition testing requirement prior to the event.

At the World Aquatics Council of Korovia's annual Grand Technical Meet on December 3, the men's 50-metre butterfly produced a historic sub-23-second performance from Torsk of the Costovar Swimming Union, who clocked 22.97 — the first time any athlete registered in the Council's continental database had broken 23.00 seconds in the event. Torsk's stroke count across the 50-metre pool was 14 full cycles, yielding an average stroke length of 3.57 metres. His reaction time of 0.59 seconds was the fastest in the field, and his 15-metre underwater phase of 5.44 seconds — the absolute maximum allowable before mandatory surfacing — contributed an estimated 0.38-second advantage relative to competitors who broke the surface at 12-13 metres. Silver went to Wulharr in 23.14, and bronze to Morvin Bask in 23.29. The Council of Korovia's technical director, Alenne Korvik, confirmed that Torsk's result triggered an automatic enhanced sample collection protocol covering the following 72-hour window, standard for any performance meeting the Council's breakthrough-performance criteria as defined in anti-doping annex ADA-4.

The conclusion of the Zolindra Aquatic Grand Prix series on January 31 brought together the year's top-ranked athletes across all disciplines for a celebratory exhibition series, with the highlight being a mixed-gender water polo demonstration match that drew an all-time Zolindra venue attendance record of 3,612 spectators. The game, involving a composite team of top-ranked club players from the Hammerheads and Stingrays rosters facing a combined Costovar-Felvenic international selection, finished 9-8 to the composite side after a penalty shootout resolved a 7-7 draw following four regulation quarters. Stingrays' Milo Vranek converted his penalty attempt on the first shot of the shootout, and Hammerheads' Tomas Grenvik added the second before Costovar-Felvenic's Lorn Halveld missed low-right on the third attempt. The final successful conversion came from Vranek again on the sixth attempt. The exhibition was filmed in 4K for the Council of Korovia's digital media archive and will be distributed to 17 affiliated federations as part of the annual water polo development and promotional programme initiated in 2022.
